title: A useful skill you only learn outside school
summary: Argue for one practical skill that school never teaches but that life eventually forces you to learn.
topic: A useful skill you only learn outside school
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing for one specific skill that schools rarely teach but that adults end up needing. Be concrete: describe a moment when you, or someone you watched, had to learn it the hard way. Use opinion phrases, modal verbs, and at least one second-conditional sentence.
hintStarters: What strikes me about this skill is…
I'd argue that nobody really learns it until…
If schools taught this earlier, we would…

hintVocab.meaningVi: phản đối một cách lịch sự

hintVocab.phrase: to walk away from a deal

sampleText:
The most useful skill I have ever learned is how to negotiate, and no school I went to ever taught it. I picked it up the hard way, in a small electronics market in Istanbul, when I was twenty-two and trying to buy a second-hand camera. The seller named a price; I nodded, paid, and walked out feeling clever. Two stalls down, the same model was a third cheaper. I'd argue that this kind of moment teaches you more than a whole year of school.

What strikes me about negotiation is that it isn't really about money. It's about staying calm while someone tests you. If schools taught this earlier, we would grow up less afraid of disagreement. A teenager who can ask for a fair price can also ask a professor for an extension, push back politely on a bad shift at work, or walk away from a deal that smells wrong. These are the conversations adults have every week, and most of us improvise them badly. We either give in too fast or get angry and lose face. Both come from the same problem: nobody taught us the middle path, where you stay friendly but firm.

Some people will say that negotiation is a personality trait, not a skill. I disagree. I have watched shy friends turn into careful, patient negotiators after a few months of practice in flea markets in Berlin and night markets in Bangkok. They learned to pause, to ask one more question, to read the room before naming a number. Schools could practise this with role-plays — renting a flat, asking for a raise, splitting a bill with friends who earn very different salaries. None of it needs special equipment. It only needs a teacher who treats disagreement as normal, not rude. If we did that, fewer adults would feel that every difficult conversation is a small emergency, and more of us would understand that a good deal usually leaves both sides slightly uncomfortable, and that this discomfort is the price of being fair.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: hỏi một mức giá hợp lý
phản đối một cách lịch sự
coi sự bất đồng là điều bình thường

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for hypothetical result
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If schools taught this earlier, we would grow up less afraid…' dùng quá khứ đơn + would để nói về một tình huống giả định ở hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If schools taught this earlier, we would grow up less afraid…' uses past simple + would to describe a hypothetical present situation.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have watched shy friends turn into…' dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để mô tả kinh nghiệm tích lũy qua thời gian, không gắn với một thời điểm cụ thể.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have watched shy friends turn into…' uses the present perfect to describe experience accumulated over time, not tied to a specific moment.

sampleTranslationVi:
Kỹ năng hữu ích nhất tôi từng học là đàm phán, và không trường nào tôi từng học dạy điều đó. Tôi học nó theo cách khó khăn, trong một khu chợ điện tử nhỏ ở Istanbul, khi tôi hai mươi hai tuổi và đang tìm mua một chiếc máy ảnh cũ. Người bán ra giá; tôi gật đầu, trả tiền, và bước ra cảm thấy mình thông minh. Hai sạp sau, cùng một mẫu rẻ hơn một phần ba. Tôi cho rằng khoảnh khắc đó dạy bạn nhiều hơn cả một năm học.

Điều làm tôi ấn tượng về đàm phán là nó không thực sự liên quan đến tiền. Nó là việc giữ bình tĩnh khi ai đó thử mình. Nếu trường học dạy điều này sớm hơn, chúng ta sẽ lớn lên ít sợ bất đồng hơn. Một thiếu niên có thể hỏi một mức giá hợp lý cũng có thể xin giáo sư gia hạn, phản đối một cách lịch sự một ca làm dở, hoặc rút khỏi một thỏa thuận có vẻ không ổn. Đây là những cuộc trò chuyện mà người lớn có mỗi tuần, và hầu hết chúng ta ứng biến rất tệ. Chúng ta hoặc nhượng bộ quá nhanh hoặc tức giận và mất mặt. Cả hai đều xuất phát từ cùng một vấn đề: chưa ai dạy ta con đường ở giữa, nơi bạn vẫn thân thiện nhưng cứng rắn.

Một số người sẽ nói rằng đàm phán là một nét tính cách, không phải một kỹ năng. Tôi không đồng ý. Tôi đã chứng kiến những người bạn rụt rè trở thành những người đàm phán cẩn thận, kiên nhẫn sau vài tháng luyện tập ở các chợ trời tại Berlin và chợ đêm tại Bangkok. Họ học cách dừng lại, hỏi thêm một câu, đọc vị bầu không khí trước khi đưa ra một con số. Trường học có thể luyện tập điều này qua đóng vai — thuê nhà, xin tăng lương, chia hóa đơn với bạn bè có mức thu nhập rất khác nhau. Không cần thiết bị đặc biệt nào. Chỉ cần một giáo viên coi sự bất đồng là điều bình thường, không thô lỗ. Nếu chúng ta làm vậy, ít người lớn cảm thấy mọi cuộc trò chuyện khó khăn là một tình huống khẩn cấp nhỏ, và nhiều người trong chúng ta sẽ hiểu rằng một thỏa thuận tốt thường khiến cả hai bên hơi khó chịu, và sự khó chịu đó là cái giá của sự công bằng.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If schools will teach this, we grow less afraid.
If schools taught this earlier, we would grow up less afraid of disagreement.
If schools teach this earlier, we would grew up less afraid.
If schools taught this, we will grow up less afraid.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional = past simple in the if-clause + 'would' + base verb in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple after 'if', then 'would' + base verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best expresses a polite, B1 opinion phrase?
options: I'd argue that this kind of moment teaches you more than a whole year of school.
I am argued that this moment teaches more.
It argues me that this moment teaches more.
I am arguing this moment teach you more.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I'd argue that…' is a natural, slightly tentative opinion phrase; the others are ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a contracted modal followed by 'argue that'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence with the correct preposition.
options: Walk away of a deal that smells wrong.
Walk away on a deal that smells wrong.
Walk away from a deal that smells wrong.
Walk away at a deal that smells wrong.
mcQuestions.explanation: The fixed phrase is 'walk away from' something.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly for accumulated experience?
options: I am watching shy friends turn into careful negotiators.
I watched shy friends turn into careful negotiators yesterday.
I have watched shy friends turn into careful, patient negotiators.
I have watch shy friends turn into careful negotiators.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect (have + past participle) signals experience built up over time, without a specific moment.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'have' + past participle and no specific time.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a modal verb most naturally to suggest a school activity?
options: Schools must to practise this with role-plays.
Schools could practise this with role-plays.
Schools are could practise this with role-plays.
Schools should to practising this with role-plays.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could + base verb' politely proposes a possible activity.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal verbs are followed by the bare infinitive.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a relative clause correctly?
options: A teenager who can ask for a fair price can also ask a professor for an extension.
A teenager which can ask for a fair price can also ask a professor.
A teenager what can ask for a fair price can also ask a professor.
A teenager who he can ask for a fair price can also ask a professor.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Who' is the correct relative pronoun for people, and no second subject is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: People take 'who' and you don't repeat the subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence has the most natural register for a B1 opinion essay?
options: Negotiation, it is a thing that we humans must utilise.
What strikes me about negotiation is that it isn't really about money.
Negotiation is dope and everyone has to learn it ASAP.
Negotiation is a thing what people they need.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What strikes me about…' is a controlled, B1-appropriate opinion frame; the others are too casual, too formal, or ungrammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a clear opinion frame without slang or broken grammar.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly answers an objection?
options: Some people negotiation is personal, but no.
Some people will say that negotiation is a personality trait, not a skill. I disagree.
Disagree I, some people will say negotiation personality.
Some people, negotiation personality trait it is, not skill.
mcQuestions.explanation: State the objection cleanly, then push back in a new sentence with 'I disagree'.
mcQuestions.hint: Two clean sentences: objection, then response.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which linker fits best at the start of a contrasting clause?
options: Because, a good deal usually leaves both sides uncomfortable.
However, a good deal usually leaves both sides slightly uncomfortable.
So that, a good deal usually leaves both sides uncomfortable.
Although a good deal usually leaves, both sides comfortable.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However' introduces a contrasting idea at the start of a sentence, followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: Which linker signals contrast and stands at the start with a comma?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural B1 way to describe an ability.
options: They learned how to pause, ask one more question, and read the room.
They learned how pause, asking one question, and read room.
They learn how to paused, ask one more question, read the room.
They are learning how to paused and asked one question.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Learn how to' + base verb, with parallel verbs joined by commas and 'and'.

options: It really is about money not.
Really it about money is not.
It isn't really about money.
It is about money really not.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Really' usually sits between the auxiliary and the main idea: 'isn't really about money'.
mcQuestions.hint: Adverbs of degree often follow the auxiliary verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Which 'outside-school' skill do you think your parents' generation valued more than yours does today, and is that a loss or a gain?
